answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, though not much in the exact form of “people who chose the more interesting FAANG job later regretted it less.” The strongest evidence is broader: what predicts job satisfaction, well-being, burnout, and turnover.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and labor economics, the most consistent finding is:

**Interesting/meaningful work, autonomy, learning, and fit with your values tend to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than salary does.**

Salary matters, but usually **less** than people expect once it is already high enough to remove financial stress and feels fair/competitive.

For your specific tradeoff, the literature would usually predict:

- **More interesting work** → higher day-to-day satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover risk
- **More money** → some increase in satisfaction, but typically modest unless it materially changes your life
- **Leading a team** → can increase satisfaction *if the actual work of management* fits you; otherwise it often adds stress and reduces enjoyment, especially for people who mainly love the technical/research part

So the research-based answer is not “always stay in the more interesting job,” but rather:

> **Interesting work usually beats a modest pay increase.**
>  
> **A leadership role can beat interesting work only if leadership itself is intrinsically rewarding to you, not just attractive in status/title.**

---

## What the research says

### 1. Job design matters a lot: interesting work is not a minor perk

A large literature going back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** found that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity** (seeing a whole piece of work)
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are very close to what people usually mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  Found that work characteristics like **autonomy, task significance, and variety** were strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - engagement
  - performance
  - lower burnout/strain

This is one of the most robust findings in work psychology: **how the work feels day to day** matters a lot.

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a large “meaningful work” literature.

Meta-analytic work by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** found that **meaningful work** is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **burnout** / psychological strain

In plain language: people who feel their work is worth doing and mentally engaging tend to do better psychologically.

That does **not** prove causality in every case, but across many studies the relationship is very consistent.

### 3. Autonomy and competence are central

**Self-Determination Theory** is another major research tradition here. It argues that people function best when work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have agency
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — connection to others

Reviews of workplace research (e.g. **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan**) find that jobs satisfying these needs are linked to:

- better motivation
- more persistence
- higher well-being
- lower burnout

For a research engineer, “interesting work” often packages exactly these things:
- curiosity
- mastery
- non-routine problems
- creative freedom

That combination tends to be psychologically valuable.

---

## Salary: important, but usually weaker than people think

### 4. Pay is related to job satisfaction, but only modestly

Research on the pay–job satisfaction link consistently finds that the relationship exists, but it is **much smaller** than the relationship with factors like autonomy, meaningfulness, or fit.

A commonly cited meta-analytic conclusion from **Judge and colleagues** is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest** — roughly in the **small** range, not large.

That means:
- more pay generally helps
- but pay alone explains surprisingly little of why some people like their jobs and others don’t

Why? Because people adapt, and because pay is often less important than:
- whether the job is interesting
- whether it is draining
- whether the environment is good
- whether the role fits the person

### 5. Pay matters more when it changes your life materially

The literature suggests money matters more when it affects:
- financial security
- debt
- family flexibility
- housing
- ability to say no to bad situations
- perceived fairness

So a “bit higher salary” is not the same as a life-changing jump.

If your current compensation is already very high, the research would usually predict **diminishing marginal returns** from additional pay, especially for day-to-day emotional well-being.

Related income research (e.g. **Kahneman & Deaton**, later **Killingsworth**) suggests that income does improve well-being, but the effect is more nuanced than “more money = proportionally more happiness forever.” Also, income often affects **overall life evaluation** more than **daily moment-to-moment experience**.

That distinction matters here because jobs are lived daily.

---

## Leadership: this is the wildcard

### 6. Wanting to lead can be a real source of satisfaction — if you actually like management work

You said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.” Research would take that seriously.

A lot of job satisfaction comes from **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit**.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  Found that better fit is strongly associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher organizational commitment
  - lower turnover intention

So if becoming a lead/manager matches a deep personal goal, that is not a trivial factor. It could genuinely improve satisfaction.

But the key research-based caveat is:

> People are often attracted to leadership as an identity or achievement, but the actual day-to-day work of management is very different from the idea of being a leader.

Management usually means more of:
- coaching
- hiring
- feedback
- performance conversations
- prioritization
- politics/cross-functional alignment
- less uninterrupted deep work

Some people love that. Some do not.

### 7. Management often brings both more autonomy and more strain

The evidence here is mixed, because management roles come with both gains and costs:

**Possible gains**
- more status
- more influence
- more autonomy/control
- broader impact
- development of a valued identity

**Possible costs**
- longer hours
- role overload
- emotional labor
- administrative work
- less direct technical/research time
- responsibility for other people’s outcomes

So the research does **not** support a simple claim like “people are happier when promoted into management.”

The better-supported claim is:

> Management is satisfying when it fits the person’s motives and strengths; otherwise it can reduce well-being even if it increases status and pay.

That is especially relevant for highly technical people. Some enjoy multiplying others; others miss the craft.

---

## Regret: is there evidence on what people later wish they had done?

There is **less direct research on regret after job choices** than on satisfaction while in the job.

But a few related findings are relevant.

### 8. People often overweight extrinsic rewards and underweight daily experience

Research on **affective forecasting** and **miswanting** suggests people are not great at predicting what will make them happy. They tend to overestimate the long-run effect of:
- money
- prestige
- titles

and underestimate the importance of:
- daily tasks
- social environment
- autonomy
- interest

Applied here, that means people may overvalue:
- “I’d be leading a team at Meta”
- “the comp is higher”

and undervalue:
- “how do I actually feel from 10am to 6pm doing the work?”

That’s not a moral claim; it’s a forecasting-bias claim.

### 9. Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals

Related research from **Kasser & Ryan** and others on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations finds that prioritizing intrinsic goals (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) is generally associated with better well-being than prioritizing extrinsic goals (money, status, image) as ends in themselves.

This does **not** mean money/status are bad. It means that when a choice is primarily driven by extrinsic reward, it tends to predict lower well-being than choices aligned with intrinsic motivation.

In your case:
- **interesting work** is often an intrinsic good
- **team leadership** can also be an intrinsic good, if it expresses growth/impact
- **higher salary** is mostly extrinsic

So the crucial question is whether the Meta offer is really about money/status, or about a genuine intrinsic aspiration to build and lead.

---

## Important caveat: interesting work is not always better

There is one important complication.

### 10. Highly interesting or meaningful work can also increase overwork and burnout

Research on **calling**, **passion**, and identity-based work shows a double edge:

People who find work highly meaningful often report:
- more engagement
- more satisfaction

but they may also:
- work longer hours
- accept worse boundaries
- tolerate stress
- become overidentified with work

There is a distinction in passion research:
- **harmonious passion** → interest integrated into life, usually healthier
- **obsessive passion** → compulsion, difficulty disengaging, more burnout risk

So if your OpenAI role is “very interesting” but also all-consuming, that matters.

The best predictor is not just “interesting vs less interesting,” but:
- interesting **and sustainable**
vs
- interesting **and chronically depleting**

---

## What the evidence suggests for your exact dilemma

If I translate the literature into your tradeoff, without giving personal advice:

### The research would generally favor staying if:
- the OpenAI work is substantially more interesting/meaningful
- you have strong autonomy and learning there
- the pay difference is not life-changing
- the Meta role is mainly attractive for title/advancement rather than the actual managerial work
- you suspect you would miss hands-on research a lot

### The research would generally favor switching if:
- leading people is itself deeply motivating for you
- the Meta role gives meaningful autonomy and scope, not just more admin
- you want to optimize for broader impact through others rather than direct technical work
- the pay increase materially changes your life or future options
- the OpenAI role is interesting but unsustainably intense / poor for long-term well-being

---

## The strongest empirical summary I can give

If I had to compress the research into one sentence:

> **On average, people are happier in jobs that are interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and aligned with their values than in jobs that simply pay a bit more.**

And then one important qualifier:

> **But if leadership is itself a core source of meaning for you, a management role can be the better choice despite less interesting technical content.**

So the central research question is probably **not** “interesting work vs salary,” but:

> **Which role better satisfies your intrinsic motives: curiosity/mastery, or leadership/impact-through-others?**

Because both of those can beat money.

---

## If you want papers to actually read

A good starting list would be:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person–environment fit
- **Judge, T. A. et al.** — meta-analytic work on pay and job satisfaction
- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M. (2017)** — Self-Determination Theory in work organizations
- **Allan, B. A. et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being
- **Wrzesniewski et al.** — jobs, careers, and callings
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

If you want, I can give you a **more rigorous annotated bibliography** with:
1. strongest meta-analyses,  
2. likely effect sizes, and  
3. which findings are causal vs merely correlational.

answer:
Yes. There is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not many studies that ask your exact question in the exact context of elite research jobs.

The short version is:

- **The nature of the work itself**—especially whether it is interesting, autonomous, challenging, and meaningful—is one of the **strongest predictors of job satisfaction**.
- **Pay matters**, but once compensation is already high, its relationship to job satisfaction is **much weaker** than many people expect.
- **Leadership/promotion opportunities** can increase satisfaction for some people, but the average effect is **mixed and often temporary**, because promotions also bring stress, admin, and less hands-on work.
- On **regret**, the literature suggests that in the long run people often regret **inaction** more than action—but whether that points toward Meta or OpenAI depends on which unrealized self would haunt you more: “I never tried leading” vs “I gave up uniquely interesting work.”

## 1) How important is “interesting work”?

The strongest evidence comes from organizational psychology on **job design**.

### Job characteristics research
The classic model is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. The basic idea is that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity**
- **Task significance**
- **Autonomy**
- **Feedback**

Meta-analytic work since then has generally supported this. A particularly influential review is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design

Their conclusion, broadly, is that jobs high in motivational characteristics—especially **autonomy**, meaningfulness-related features, and feedback—are strongly associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal work motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**

That maps pretty directly onto what people usually mean by “interesting work.”

### Meaningful work
Related literature on **meaningful work** finds similar results:

- Meaningful work is positively associated with **job satisfaction**, **work engagement**, and often **general well-being**
- It is negatively associated with **turnover intentions** and sometimes with **burnout**, though the relationship there is more complicated

A useful reference is:

- **Allan et al. (2019)**, meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, challenging, and meaningful,” the literature is quite consistent: **people in such jobs are, on average, more satisfied with their work**.

## 2) How does that compare with salary?

This is one of the clearest findings.

### Pay has a real but modest link to job satisfaction
A major meta-analysis found that the correlation between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**.

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

The usual summary is: **money matters, but much less than people think**—especially once income is already comfortably high.

That does **not** mean salary is irrelevant. It affects:

- financial stress
- life options
- household stability
- location choices
- perceived status/fairness

But for **day-to-day job happiness**, the content of the work tends to matter much more.

### Income and overall well-being
Broader well-being research also shows:

- Higher income tends to improve **life evaluation**
- The effect on **moment-to-moment emotional well-being** is smaller
- At already-high incomes, the **marginal gain per extra dollar** is usually limited compared with changes in daily work experience

The exact “plateau” story is debated, but the broad point remains: in a comparison like yours—already high-status, high-paying roles—the additional salary is unlikely to dominate the effect of a major change in **how your days feel**.

## 3) Intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation

Another highly relevant literature is **Self-Determination Theory**.

This research consistently finds that people do better psychologically when work supports:

- **Autonomy** — feeling you choose what you’re doing
- **Competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **Relatedness** — feeling connected to others

These factors are strongly linked to:

- greater well-being
- better motivation
- less burnout
- better persistence

In plain language: jobs that are intrinsically engaging tend to be better for well-being than jobs pursued mainly for extrinsic rewards like pay or prestige.

That does **not** mean extrinsic rewards are bad. It means that if one role is clearly better on intrinsic motivation and the other is only somewhat better on extrinsic rewards, the research usually leans toward the intrinsically better role.

## 4) What about leadership? Could that outweigh “interesting work”?

Yes, potentially.

This is where the answer becomes less one-dimensional.

If leading a team is something you have “always wanted,” then the Meta option may not be just “more money + less interesting work.” It may also offer:

- greater **autonomy**
- more **responsibility**
- more **social impact**
- stronger fit with an emerging professional identity
- satisfaction of a genuine aspiration

That connects to **person-job fit** research.

### Person-job fit matters a lot
Meta-analyses on **person-environment fit** show that when people’s jobs match their interests, values, and preferred ways of working, they tend to have:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to quit

A standard reference is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit

So the key question is not just “interesting research vs less interesting research.”
It is also:

- Are you fundamentally more of an **individual contributor researcher**?
- Or are you increasingly someone who wants to **build, lead, and coordinate others**?

If leadership itself is deeply motivating for you, then it belongs on the **intrinsic** side of the ledger, not just the extrinsic side.

## 5) Do promotions and leadership roles actually make people happier?

The evidence is mixed.

Longitudinal studies on promotions tend to find:

- a **short-term bump** in job satisfaction after promotion
- substantial **adaptation** over time
- sometimes **higher stress** or workload afterward

That is: promotions are often less transformational than people imagine.

This is especially relevant for technical people moving into management. Many discover that leadership gives them things they wanted—scope, influence, mentoring—but also takes away things they liked—deep focus, direct craft, personal output.

So the research suggests:

- If leadership is genuinely part of your ideal work, it can increase satisfaction
- If leadership is mainly attractive as status or an abstract aspiration, the happiness gains may fade quickly

## 6) What does regret research say?

This literature is not specific to careers, but it’s still useful.

### Action vs inaction regret
A classic finding is:

- In the **short term**, people often regret **actions** more
- In the **long term**, people more often regret **inactions**

Key references:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994, 1995)**

Related work finds that major life regrets often center on domains like:

- education
- career
- relationships

In career terms, people often regret not pursuing meaningful opportunities, not trying, or drifting into choices that were safe but not aligned.

But this does **not** straightforwardly imply “take the Meta offer.” It depends on which non-chosen path feels more like the unrealized self.

Your likely regret question is probably:

- “Will I regret **not trying leadership** when I had the chance?”
or
- “Will I regret **leaving unusually interesting work** for a role I knew was less compelling day-to-day?”

Research can tell you that **foregone identity-relevant opportunities** often generate regret. It cannot tell you which identity is more central for you.

## 7) So, what does the research imply for your exact tradeoff?

If I translate the evidence into your case as cleanly as possible:

### The literature leans toward staying if:
- the OpenAI role is **substantially more interesting day-to-day**
- you already earn well above any financial stress threshold
- the Meta difference is mostly **salary + title**
- you strongly value hands-on research and would lose a lot of it

Why? Because the research says the **content and quality of daily work** tends to matter more for job satisfaction than extra pay.

### The literature leans toward Meta if:
- leading people is not just a prestige move but a **deeply valued form of work** for you
- the team-lead role satisfies an important growth motive or identity shift
- you suspect you would later regret **never testing yourself as a leader**
- the current role, however interesting, is less aligned with where you want your career to go

Why? Because then the Meta job may score higher not only on extrinsic rewards, but also on **fit**, **autonomy**, and **meaning through leadership**.

## 8) One important complication: interesting work can also be costly

There is also research showing that highly meaningful or identity-rich work can have downsides:

- people tolerate worse conditions when they believe the work is important
- “passion” can coexist with overwork and burnout
- high-interest jobs can blur boundaries and consume identity

So if the OpenAI role is more interesting but also more exhausting, volatile, or unsustainable, that matters. The literature would not say “interesting always wins.” It says interesting work is powerful **conditional on the rest of the work environment being acceptable**.

## 9) My best evidence-based synthesis

If you want the closest thing to a research-backed answer, it is this:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Extra salary has a real but much smaller effect**, especially at already-high incomes.
3. **Leadership opportunities matter when they reflect true person-job fit**, not just status.
4. **Promotions often produce adaptation**; the lasting benefit is less the title and more whether the new work itself suits you.
5. **Long-term regret often centers on unrealized possibilities**, especially identity-relevant ones.

So the question the literature would push you toward is not:
> “Which job is objectively better?”

It is:
> “Which work mode is more central to my long-term fit: doing unusually interesting research myself, or leading others even if the subject matter is less interesting?”

That is where the research narrows the problem.

## Useful references

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976, 1980). Job Characteristics Model.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007). *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010). *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.*
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005). *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*
- **Allan, B. A., et al.** (2019). Meta-analytic work on meaningful work and related outcomes.
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** (1994, 1995). Work on long-term regret and action vs inaction.
- Self-Determination Theory reviews/meta-analyses on work motivation, e.g. **Van den Broeck** and colleagues.

If you want, I can do a second pass and turn this into a **decision matrix based only on research-backed predictors**—for example, weighting interest, autonomy, leadership fit, compensation, burnout risk, and regret likelihood.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, though it usually studies **“interesting work” indirectly** via constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job characteristics** (variety, autonomy, challenge, feedback)
- **meaningful work**
- **person–job fit / interest congruence**

The broad finding is fairly consistent:

## Bottom line from the literature

**The intrinsic quality of the work itself** — especially whether it is interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and uses your strengths — is usually a **stronger predictor of job satisfaction and often mental well-being** than **pay level alone**, once pay is already reasonably high/fair.

A modest salary increase usually helps **less** than a meaningful change in the nature of the work.

Direct research on **“regret”** about choosing interesting work over higher pay is much thinner. But the best indirect evidence suggests that people are generally better off when their jobs satisfy intrinsic motives than when they mainly optimize for money/status.

---

## What the strongest evidence says

### 1) Job design matters a lot
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later backed by large meta-analyses.

**Key result:** jobs with more
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **complexity/challenge**

are associated with substantially higher **job satisfaction**, **internal motivation**, and lower **turnover intentions**.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design research  
  Found that motivational job characteristics had **moderate to strong** associations with job satisfaction and motivation.  
  In practical terms: the design of the work itself matters a lot.

This is probably the closest literature to your “interesting vs less interesting job” question.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
Another large literature studies **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Meaningful work was strongly associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - better **life satisfaction**
  - lower **burnout/distress**

This is important because “interesting” work often overlaps with work that feels **meaningful**, even if not perfectly.

---

### 3) Autonomy, competence, and relatedness at work are central
From **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan), the most important ingredients for healthy motivation are:

- **autonomy** — feeling you choose how to work
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected/respected

Across many studies and reviews, jobs that satisfy these needs produce better:
- well-being
- engagement
- persistence
- performance quality

This matters because “interesting work” often brings more **autonomy** and **competence satisfaction**, while some management roles can increase status/pay but reduce day-to-day intrinsic engagement.

---

### 4) Pay matters, but less than many people think for job satisfaction
The evidence on pay is fairly robust:

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction**  
  The relationship exists, but it is **small** relative to many job-design variables.

The rough takeaway from that literature is:

- Pay is **not irrelevant**
- But **absolute pay level** is usually a **surprisingly weak predictor** of job satisfaction compared with:
  - whether the work is interesting
  - whether you have autonomy
  - whether the job fits you
  - whether the work feels meaningful
  - whether your manager/context is good

A useful nuance:
- **Pay fairness** and **pay relative to expectations/peers** often matter more psychologically than raw salary.

So a **bit higher salary** may matter less than you’d expect if your current pay already feels good and fair.

---

### 5) Fit between your interests and the job matters
There is also a literature on **person–job fit** and **interest congruence**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  Better fit between the person and the job/organization predicts:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - stronger commitment
  - lower intention to quit

More specifically, vocational interest-congruence research generally finds that when people work in roles aligned with what they actually enjoy, they are more satisfied and more likely to persist.

The effect is usually **real but somewhat smaller** than the pure job-design effects above. Still, it points in the same direction: **fit matters**.

---

### 6) A “better” job on paper is not always better for mental health
There is a useful result from labor/mental-health research:

- **Butterworth et al. (2011)**, longitudinal panel study  
  Found that **poor-quality jobs** (low control, high demands, insecurity, unfairness) could be **as bad as or worse than unemployment** for mental health.

That doesn’t mean your Meta offer is a bad job, obviously. The point is narrower:

> The quality of the work experience matters more than the simple fact of more status, a new title, or higher pay.

In other words, moving into a role that is less interesting and less intrinsically satisfying is not automatically “worth it” just because it pays more or sounds more senior.

---

## What about leadership roles specifically?

This part of the evidence is less clean.

Research on **promotion/leadership** suggests something like:

- promotions often raise **income** and sometimes **job satisfaction** in the short run
- but gains in **overall life satisfaction** are often **small, mixed, or short-lived**
- and leadership often comes with:
  - more stress
  - more hours
  - more conflict/coordination load
  - less hands-on work

So if what you want is specifically **to lead a team**, that is a real source of value — but the literature does **not** say “more senior = happier.” It’s more conditional than that.

A key practical distinction is:

- Do you want **leadership because you deeply want to build/mentor/decide**?
- Or do you want leadership because it feels like the next prestigious step?

The research is much friendlier to the first motivation than the second.

---

## Does choosing interesting work lead to less regret?

This is the part where the evidence is weakest.

I don’t know of a strong body of prospective research that directly asks:

> “People who stayed in the more interesting job rather than taking the higher-paid leadership role — do they regret it less 5–10 years later?”

That exact question is not well studied.

But there are two relevant indirect literatures:

### A) Intrinsic vs extrinsic values
Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and others finds that people who prioritize **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, meaning, mastery) tend to report better well-being than those who prioritize **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status) more heavily.

That doesn’t mean money/status are bad. It means that **organizing your decisions mainly around them** is usually not the best recipe for well-being.

### B) Affective forecasting / adaptation
People often **overestimate** the lasting happiness gains from external rewards like:
- salary increases
- status
- prestige
- titles

and **underestimate** the enduring importance of daily experience:
- Is the work engaging?
- Do I like spending my days this way?
- Am I learning?
- Do I respect the people I work with?
- Do I want to keep doing this on a Tuesday, not just on LinkedIn?

This is one of the more durable findings in happiness research generally.

---

## If you want a very compressed research summary

If I had to summarize the literature in one paragraph:

> For people who are already well paid, the strongest and most consistent predictors of job satisfaction are not small differences in salary, but the intrinsic properties of the work: autonomy, challenge, variety, meaning, fit, and good social context. These factors also spill over into broader well-being. Promotions and leadership roles can be rewarding, but they do not reliably increase overall happiness and often bring extra strain. There is little direct regret research on your exact tradeoff, but the indirect evidence favors choosing work that remains intrinsically engaging unless the alternative meaningfully improves other dimensions you care about.

---

## The most relevant papers/results to look at

If you want to read the literature directly, I’d start with:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Strongest source on job design and satisfaction.

2. **Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
   *Motivation through the design of work*  
   Foundational theory behind “interesting work.”

3. **Judge et al. (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
   Good antidote to overestimating salary effects.

4. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis*  
   Best source on meaning and well-being.

5. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit*  
   Relevant if you think in terms of fit.

6. **Butterworth et al. (2011)**  
   *The psychosocial quality of work determines whether employment has benefits for mental health*  
   Useful reminder that job quality matters deeply.

7. **Kasser & Ryan** (1990s work on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations)  
   Relevant for the “money/status vs interesting/meaningful work” axis.

---

## My best evidence-based answer to your actual question

If your choice is:

- **Current job**: more interesting, already very well paid
- **New job**: a bit more pay, leadership opportunity, but much less interesting

then the research would generally predict:

- **higher day-to-day job satisfaction** from the more interesting role
- likely **better overall well-being** if the interesting role also gives autonomy/meaning/growth
- **smaller-than-expected benefit** from the extra salary
- **uncertain net well-being gain** from the leadership role unless leading itself is intrinsically important to you

So the literature does **not** support the naive idea that “higher pay + leadership title” will usually make people happier if the work becomes much less engaging.

What it does support is:
- **interesting/autonomous/meaningful work matters a lot**
- **salary matters, but modest increments matter less**
- **leadership can be great if you truly want the activity of leading**
- direct **regret** evidence is sparse

If you want, I can turn this into a more rigorous **one-page literature memo** with citations and a short “decision implications” section.

Plus, I can offer to help create a decision matrix if they want!
**Summarizing job satisfaction factors**

I’m thinking I could phrase my insights as "Broadly, yes." In job satisfaction surveys, the "nature of work" stands out as one of the highest-rated aspects, while pay shows only a modest correlation. I should break down "interestingness" into components like variety, challenge, learning, meaning, and autonomy. It's important to note that while job satisfaction can predict turnover and some aspects of life satisfaction, it's not the same as overall happiness, especially considering cross-domain spillover. Maybe I'll draft this with bullets and a compact table!
**Exploring management effects**

Meaningful work might correlate around .4 to .6 with overall job satisfaction and life meaning, which negatively affects turnover intentions. I should emphasize these correlations as being moderate. answer:
Short version: **yes, there is fairly strong research that the “interestingness” of work—usually studied as intrinsic motivation, meaningfulness, autonomy, skill variety, challenge, and growth—matters a lot for job satisfaction and well-being, often more than pay once pay is already good.** But the literature is much stronger on **satisfaction/well-being** than on **long-run regret** specifically.

A few robust findings:

## 1) The nature of the work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually broken into components like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn and grow**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses. A major review/meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these work-design features are strongly associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal work motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **burnout / strain** in many cases

In plain English: people tend to be happier in jobs that feel mentally engaging, meaningful, and self-directed.

## 2) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is weaker than many people expect
A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge and colleagues** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. The correlation is not zero, but it is much smaller than many people intuitively expect.

That fits a broader pattern in happiness research:

- **Income strongly matters when it reduces financial stress / insecurity**
- once you’re already well compensated, **additional money still helps, but with diminishing returns**
- non-monetary job features often explain more of day-to-day work happiness than incremental pay differences

So if the choice is between “already very good pay + very interesting work” and “slightly higher pay + less interesting work,” the literature generally does **not** suggest that the extra pay will reliably compensate for losing intrinsic quality of the work.

## 3) Meaningful work is consistently linked to better well-being
There is a large literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic work (for example, **Allan and colleagues**) generally finds that people who experience their work as meaningful report higher:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction
- organizational commitment

and lower:

- turnover intentions
- cynicism / burnout

Important nuance: “meaningful” is not exactly the same as “interesting,” but in practice they often overlap for knowledge workers.

## 4) Autonomy is especially important
From **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) and related workplace research, three needs repeatedly matter for well-being:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that satisfy these needs produce better motivation and well-being. For many research/engineering roles, “interesting work” often means high autonomy + competence growth + challenging problems. Those are exactly the ingredients the theory predicts will support well-being.

This matters for your case because a team-leading role could go either way:

- it may increase **autonomy**, influence, status, growth
- but decrease direct contact with the work you find intrinsically engaging

So “leadership opportunity” is not purely an external factor. It may itself be intrinsically valuable if it fulfills a longstanding goal.

## 5) People do trade money for intrinsically better jobs
Labor economics has a related idea: workers accept lower wages for desirable **nonpecuniary job attributes**. Economists call these **compensating differentials**.

This isn’t exactly happiness research, but it is relevant. It implies that, in aggregate, workers value things like:

- interesting work
- flexibility
- meaning
- autonomy
- status
- location
- safety

enough to give up some salary for them. So revealed preference data are broadly consistent with the psych literature: job quality is not “just fluff.”

## 6) On regret specifically: the evidence is much thinner
I’m not aware of a strong body of research that directly tracks people who chose “more interesting work over more money” and then measures regret years later in a clean causal way.

What we do have is more indirect:

### a) Regret research often finds that long-term regrets are more about missed personally meaningful opportunities
Classic regret research by **Gilovich & Medvec** suggests that over time, **regrets of inaction** often loom larger than regrets of action.

Related work by **Roese & Summerville** found that common life regrets cluster around areas like:

- education
- career
- romance

and often concern failures to pursue one’s “ideal self” or important aspirations.

That doesn’t mean “always choose the interesting job.” But it does suggest that people are often haunted more by **not pursuing what mattered deeply to them** than by not maximizing an already-good payoff.

### b) But meaningful work can also lead people to tolerate bad conditions
There’s also research on the “dark side” of calling/meaningful work: people sometimes accept:

- overwork
- lower pay
- weak boundaries
- burnout

because the work feels important.

So “interesting” is not automatically better if it comes with chronic overload, instability, or poor management.

---

# What the research seems to say overall

If I had to compress the literature into a few evidence-based takeaways:

### Fairly well supported
- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay is positively related to satisfaction, but usually less strongly than intrinsic job characteristics once pay is already high enough.**
- **Growth, learning, and fit with personal values matter a lot.**

### Less well supported / harder to answer cleanly
- Whether people later **regret** choosing the more interesting job over the higher-status/higher-pay one
- Whether a move into management improves well-being on average for technical people specifically

---

# For your situation, one research-based reframing
The literature would probably say your decision is **not** just:

- interesting work vs more money

It’s more like:

- **interesting technical/research work**
vs
- **leadership opportunity + somewhat more money + less interesting work**

And from a research standpoint, both “interesting work” and “getting to lead a team, if leadership is a core aspiration” are **intrinsic goods**, not just external rewards.

So the relevant empirical question is not only “how much does interesting work matter?” but also:

- How much of your well-being comes from **problem interest**?
- How much would you gain from **autonomy/influence/leadership identity**?
- How much of the salary difference is actually meaningful at your current income level?
- Will the Meta role reduce the parts of work that currently produce flow/engagement?
- Can you get a leadership path without giving up the more interesting work?

That last one matters a lot because the literature on **person-job fit** suggests that the best outcomes usually come from fit between the role and what you personally value most.

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision framework** with weighted factors and evidence strength, rather than giving personal advice.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**The day-to-day quality of the work itself—especially interest, meaning, autonomy, learning, and fit—tends to matter a lot for job satisfaction and well-being, often more than moderate differences in pay.**

That does **not** mean salary and advancement are unimportant. It means that, once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is low, the marginal well-being benefit of “more pay” is usually smaller than people expect, while the content of the job remains a strong predictor of how they feel.

A concise research summary:

## 1) “Interesting/meaningful work” is strongly associated with happiness at work

A lot of this comes from organizational psychology.

### Job characteristics research
The classic model here is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analytic work such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

The work characteristics most reliably linked to satisfaction/motivation are things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/grow**

These are very close to what many people mean by “interesting work.”

Across studies, these job-design variables tend to show **moderate to strong associations** with:
- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

In plain English: people generally do better when their work is mentally engaging, consequential, and self-directed.

## 2) Meaningful work predicts well-being, not just performance

There’s also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Reviews/meta-analyses in this area generally find that people who experience their work as meaningful report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- better general well-being
- lower burnout
- lower intention to quit

The effect sizes are not trivial; they are usually much larger than the typical effect of pay level alone on job satisfaction.

A useful nuance: “interesting” and “meaningful” are related but not identical.
- A job can be intellectually interesting but not meaningful.
- A job can be meaningful but not very stimulating.
- The best outcomes usually come when people have **both**.

## 3) Pay matters—but less than most people think for satisfaction

A very relevant result here is the meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on **pay and job satisfaction**.

Their bottom line was that the relationship between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with their job** is **positive but modest**. The correlation is often summarized as roughly **small** by psychology standards.

That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It means:

- pay matters a lot when it affects security, status, or fairness
- but after a certain point, **more money does not buy proportionally more job satisfaction**
- other things often matter more: autonomy, interesting tasks, quality of management, growth, fit, and meaning

Also, **perceived fairness of pay** often matters more psychologically than the absolute amount.

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to be better for well-being than extrinsic rewards

This is one of the central claims of **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; workplace review by **Gagné & Deci, 2005**).

Work is generally better for well-being when it satisfies:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A job that is interesting usually feeds autonomy and competence. A team leadership role may also feed competence and relatedness—**if you actually want to lead** and not just “should” lead.

A common research finding: people who pursue work mainly for **intrinsic reasons** (interest, growth, meaning, mastery) tend to report better well-being than those driven mainly by **extrinsic reasons** (money, prestige, image), especially when income is already comfortable.

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another strong line of research is **person-job fit** and **interest congruence**.

Meta-analytic work on fit (e.g. **Kristof-Brown et al.**) shows that when the job fits a person’s interests, values, and abilities, people tend to report:

- higher satisfaction
- lower stress/withdrawal
- better commitment

So the research question is not just:
> “Is interesting work better than higher salary?”

It is also:
> “Which option better fits what I actually care about now?”

If you genuinely care a lot about:
- intellectually interesting work, and
- leading a team,

then both are intrinsic factors. The tradeoff is not simply “meaning vs money.” It may be “intellectual stimulation vs leadership identity.”

## 6) What about regret?

This is the area where the evidence is less direct.

I don’t know of especially strong research saying:
> “People who choose the more interesting job later regret it less than those who choose higher pay.”

That exact question is not as well studied as job satisfaction.

But there are two relevant research traditions:

### A. Regret research: in the long run, people often regret inaction more than action
Classic work by **Gilovich & Medvec** found that over time, people more often regret the things they **didn’t do** than the things they did do.

That matters if the Meta role represents something identity-relevant like:
- “I always wanted to lead”
- “I might be passing up a formative management opportunity”

So from a regret perspective, the leadership piece may loom larger than the salary piece.

### B. Ideal-self regret
Research on regret also suggests that long-term regrets are often tied to failures to pursue one’s **ideal self** rather than short-term hedonic mistakes.

That means people may regret:
- not trying the thing they believed was part of who they wanted to become

more than they regret:
- not maximizing compensation

But this is indirect evidence, not a clean causal result about jobs specifically.

## 7) What tends to predict happiness better than salary alone?

If you want the research-based shortlist, the strongest recurring predictors are usually:

1. **Autonomy**
2. **Meaning / task significance**
3. **Interest / challenge / learning**
4. **Competence and growth**
5. **Manager and team quality**
6. **Person-job fit**
7. **Fair pay**
8. **Manageable workload**

So if one role is “less interesting” but offers:
- much more autonomy,
- stronger growth,
- better fit with your long-term identity,
- and a better manager/team setup,

it could still win on well-being.

But if the only advantages are:
- somewhat higher salary
- more status
- title inflation

while the day-to-day work is much less engaging, research would generally predict **lower day-to-day satisfaction**.

## 8) A research-based reading of your specific tradeoff

Without giving personal advice, I’d say the literature suggests:

### If OpenAI offers:
- substantially more intellectually interesting work
- stronger sense of meaning
- better learning
- more day-to-day engagement

then it likely has a strong advantage for **daily job satisfaction**.

### If Meta offers:
- a real opportunity to lead, develop people, and shape outcomes
- meaningful autonomy
- a role that fits a long-held identity goal

then that is not “just extrinsic.” It may satisfy intrinsic needs too.

That is an important distinction.

A lot depends on whether **leading a small team is itself interesting/meaningful to you**, versus merely a prestige marker.

If leadership itself is deeply motivating, then the Meta role may not actually be lower on the intrinsic dimension overall—it may just be lower on technical/research interest.

## 9) Practical conclusion from the literature

If you boil the research down:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work strongly predicts happiness at work.**
- **Pay predicts job satisfaction only modestly, especially once pay is already high.**
- **People often underestimate how much day-to-day work content matters.**
- **But growth opportunities tied to identity—like becoming a leader—can also matter a lot and may affect later regret.**

So the evidence does **not** support a simple rule like:
- “always choose the more interesting job”
or
- “always choose leadership and more pay.”

It supports a more specific claim:

> For well-being, intrinsic job quality usually beats moderate extrinsic gains.
> But leadership can itself be an intrinsic gain if it aligns with who you want to become.

---

If you want, I can give you a **more formal research memo** next: a table with factors like **pay, meaningfulness, autonomy, leadership, learning, regret risk**, and for each one summarize the relevant literature and its likely strength.

- **Person-job fit:** Strong predictor for satisfaction and retention.

I won't include full citations, but I’ll reference key studies, like Judge et al. (2010), which found a correlation of r=0.15 between pay level and job satisfaction for clarity.
**Exploring job satisfaction factors**

I’m thinking that, generally, having interesting, meaningful, and autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly than just differences in pay. Leadership opportunities can boost satisfaction too, but they really seem to help when they align with a personal desire to lead. I want to be careful and provide some evidence while keeping things manageable and clear. Let’s ensure to write this down thoughtfully!
answer:
Yes. There is quite a bit of research on the closest constructs to “interesting work”: **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics** like autonomy/variety/challenge, **meaningful work**, and **person–job fit**.

The short version from the literature is:

1. **More intrinsically interesting work is usually associated with higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is smaller than many people expect.**
3. **Leadership/promotion can improve satisfaction for some people, but the average effect is mixed because it also raises stress and demands.**
4. **Direct evidence on “later regret” is much thinner than evidence on satisfaction/well-being.**

## 1) “Interesting work” is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses.

### Main finding
Jobs tend to be more satisfying and motivating when they provide:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are basically formal versions of what people often mean by “interesting work.”

### Evidence
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** meta-analysis: the core job characteristics were positively related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** meta-analysis: motivational job characteristics like autonomy, task variety, and significance showed **moderate to strong associations** with job satisfaction and engagement.

In plain language: **people generally do better when the work itself is engaging, meaningful, and gives them room to think and act.**

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

### Main finding
People who see their work as meaningful tend to report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- more **engagement**
- less **burnout** and lower **turnover intentions**

### Evidence
Several meta-analytic reviews in the 2010s and 2020s found **fairly strong positive associations** between meaningful work and well-being-related outcomes.

So if by “interesting” you partly mean **intellectually compelling** or **important**, the research generally says that matters a lot.

## 3) Intrinsic factors usually beat extrinsic factors for happiness at work

A large body of work from **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent research distinguishes:
- **intrinsic factors**: interest, growth, learning, autonomy, mastery, purpose
- **extrinsic factors**: pay, prestige, title, external rewards

### Main finding
When basic financial needs are already well met, **intrinsic aspects of work tend to predict well-being better than additional extrinsic rewards**.

### Evidence
- Research in **Self-Determination Theory** consistently finds that satisfaction of **autonomy, competence, and relatedness** at work is linked to better well-being and less burnout.
- Studies on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations** find that people oriented more toward intrinsic goals tend to report better well-being than those centered mainly on money/status/prestige.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that, especially at already-high compensation levels, an additional pay increase often has **less impact on day-to-day satisfaction** than changes in the nature of the work.

## 4) Pay matters, but less than people think for job satisfaction

There is specific research on pay and job satisfaction.

### Main finding
The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively small**.

### Evidence
- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis: pay level and job satisfaction are related, but the effect is **small** by organizational-psychology standards.

So: a higher salary usually helps somewhat, but **it does not reliably compensate for boring work, low autonomy, poor fit, or lack of meaning**.

That is one of the clearest findings in the area.

## 5) Leadership/promotion is not straightforwardly “better”

Your case is especially interesting because the Meta role is not just “more money.” It also offers **leading a small team**, which you say is something you’ve wanted.

Research would not treat that as purely extrinsic. It can satisfy intrinsic motives too:
- growth
- autonomy
- impact
- competence
- identity

### What the literature generally finds
Promotions/leadership roles often bring:
- more pay/status
- sometimes more autonomy/control
- but also more stress, role conflict, time pressure, and administrative work

Average effects are therefore **mixed**.

Some longitudinal research suggests promotions can increase satisfaction in the short run, but benefits may fade, while stress and workload often rise.

So the relevant question is not just:
> “Is leadership better?”

It is more like:
> “Do I have a strong enough motivation-to-lead that the extra managerial load will still make the role feel more satisfying overall?”

That’s a **person–job fit** question.

## 6) Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

This may be the most important point for your exact decision.

The literature on **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** finds that people are happier and more likely to stay when their role matches:
- their interests
- values
- abilities
- motives

### Evidence
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis: better fit is associated with higher job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.

So if:
- you deeply value intellectually intense research, the OpenAI job may fit better;
- you deeply value building/leading a team, the Meta role may fit better.

And because “wanting to lead” sounds like an internal motive for you, that should not be discounted as “just status.” Research would treat that as potentially a major source of satisfaction.

## 7) What about regret?

This is where the evidence is weakest.

I’m not aware of a strong literature directly asking:
> “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/promotional one?”

Most of the literature instead measures:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover
- stress
- life satisfaction

### What related evidence suggests
Two relevant findings:

1. **People adapt to salary/status gains more than they expect.**  
   This is part of the broader literature on **hedonic adaptation** and **affective forecasting**.

2. **Repeated daily experiences matter a lot.**  
   If one job gives you better day-to-day experience—more interest, flow, autonomy, meaning—that usually has a persistent effect on experienced well-being.

So while “regret” evidence is sparse, the adjacent research suggests people may **overweight salient external differences** like pay/title and **underweight what the work actually feels like on a Tuesday afternoon**.

## 8) The strongest predictors of work happiness are usually not just “interestingness” or salary

If you wanted the most evidence-based shortlist of factors, it would probably be:

- **autonomy/control**
- **interesting/challenging work**
- **meaning/significance**
- **social environment / manager / team quality**
- **workload and stress**
- **fit with your motives and strengths**
- **pay**  
  (important, but often less decisive than the above once compensation is already high)

That last caveat matters a lot in your situation, because both jobs are presumably already at very high compensation levels.

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I compress the literature as much as possible:

- **Yes**: people in more intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous jobs tend to report **higher job satisfaction and engagement**.
- **Yes**: these effects are often **stronger than the effect of extra pay**.
- **But**: leadership opportunities can also improve well-being **if they match a genuine desire to lead**—which in your case seems relevant.
- **No clear evidence** says people systematically regret choosing interesting work; direct regret research is limited.
- The best-supported framework is: **choose the role with the better combination of intrinsic motivation, autonomy, meaning, manageable demands, and person–job fit**.

## For your specific tradeoff, the research would suggest asking

Not “interesting vs money,” but:

1. **Which role gives better day-to-day intrinsic experience?**
2. **How strong is your genuine motivation to lead?**
3. **Will the leadership role still feel meaningful once you subtract meetings, hiring, performance management, coordination, etc.?**
4. **Which environment gives more autonomy/control?**
5. **Which role has the healthier demand/resource balance?**
6. **How much of the salary difference would actually change your life, vs just change a number?**

If you want, I can also give you:
- a **short reading list** of the best papers/meta-analyses on this, or
- a **decision framework derived from the literature** rather than from opinion.

answer:
Yes. The closest research answer is:

**Across many studies, the intrinsic quality of the work itself — interestingness, autonomy, learning, meaning, using your strengths — tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than salary level does, once salary is already high enough to remove financial stress.**

There is **less direct evidence on “regret”** for this exact tradeoff, but the indirect evidence points in the same direction.

## What researchers usually measure instead of “interesting”
The literature usually breaks “interesting work” into things like:

- **Autonomy**: control over how you work
- **Skill variety / challenge**: work that uses and stretches your abilities
- **Task significance / meaning**: feeling the work matters
- **Feedback / mastery**: learning whether you’re doing well
- **Intrinsic motivation**: doing the work because it is engaging in itself

Those are very well-studied.

---

## 1) Job design matters a lot for satisfaction

A major line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses.

### Key result
Jobs higher in:
- autonomy
- variety
- significance
- feedback

are associated with substantially higher:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal/turnover intentions

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

Their overall conclusion was that these work-design variables have **moderate-to-strong relationships** with satisfaction and motivation. In practice, the correlations for things like autonomy and meaningful characteristics are often in the **roughly .3 to .5 range**, which is not trivial at all in organizational research.

**Interpretation:** if a job is more interesting in the day-to-day sense, people do tend to report being happier in it.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

A very useful result here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

### Key result
The relationship between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**. The headline number people often cite is around:

- **r ≈ .15** between pay level and job satisfaction

That is much smaller than many intrinsic job characteristics.

What tends to matter more than raw pay is:
- whether pay feels **fair**
- whether it matches expectations
- whether it removes financial stress
- how it compares to alternatives / peers

So the research does **not** say “money is irrelevant.” It says:
- money matters,
- but **salary differences alone are not strong predictors of liking your job**, especially at already high compensation levels.

---

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is now a sizable literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction / well-being
- more engagement
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions

The exact effect sizes vary by study and model, but the overall result is very consistent:
**meaningful work is one of the strongest positive correlates of work-related well-being.**

This is relevant because “interesting” often overlaps with:
- intellectually engaging
- aligned with values
- identity-relevant
- worth caring about

---

## 4) Autonomy and intrinsic motivation are central in well-being theory

A second big framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; applied to work by Deci, Olafsen, Ryan and many others).

### Core claim
Well-being is supported when work satisfies basic psychological needs for:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that are interesting, growth-oriented, and self-directed tend to satisfy these needs better than jobs chosen mainly for external rewards.

This theory has been supported across many studies in organizations:
- need satisfaction at work predicts better well-being, motivation, and persistence
- purely extrinsic motives are usually weaker predictors of sustainable satisfaction than intrinsic ones

This does **not** mean extrinsic rewards are bad. It means that if a job is high-paying but low on autonomy/interest/meaning, the extra pay often does not fully compensate psychologically.

---

## 5) Income and happiness: the effect exists, but it gets smaller relative to job quality at high incomes

The income-happiness literature is a bit messy, but the broad picture is:

- **Low income** strongly hurts well-being because of stress and lack of security.
- At **higher incomes**, additional money still helps somewhat, but the relationship is weaker and more about life evaluation than daily enjoyment.

Famous studies:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: higher income improves life evaluation; emotional well-being showed a plateau in their U.S. data
- **Killingsworth (2021)**: argued experienced well-being continues to rise with income, without a sharp plateau

These papers disagree on the exact shape, but they agree on something important for your case:
**money matters less, per extra dollar, once you’re already in a high-income range.**

So if the choice is between:
- already-excellent compensation + much more interesting work
- somewhat higher compensation + less interesting work

the literature would generally predict that the **difference in day-to-day work quality may matter more for happiness** than the additional salary.

---

## 6) “Fit” matters: if leading a team is something you deeply want, that changes the equation

This is important, because your tradeoff is not just:

- interesting work vs more money

It is also:

- individual contributor research role vs leadership role

There is strong evidence from **person-job fit / person-environment fit** research that people are happier when the role fits what they value and want to become.

Classic meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

### Key result
Better fit predicts:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better well-being

So if **leading a small team** is not just a status badge, but something you genuinely find meaningful and identity-congruent, then leadership itself may count as an **intrinsic** positive, not merely an extrinsic perk.

That is a research-based reason not to reduce the choice to “interesting work vs salary.”

For some people, managing/mentoring/building is deeply satisfying. For others, it is mostly meetings and administration. The literature says the **fit** matters a lot.

---

## 7) What about regret?

Here the evidence is weaker and more indirect.

I do **not** know of a strong body of research that directly says:
> “People who chose the more interesting job instead of the higher-paid less-interesting one later regret it less.”

That exact design is hard to study.

But there are two relevant literatures:

### A. Long-term regret research
Studies by **Gilovich**, **Medvec**, **Roese**, and others suggest that in the long run, people often regret:
- missed opportunities
- paths not taken
- choices that conflict with their “ideal self”

Career is a major regret domain in these studies.

This literature is **not** specific to interesting vs salary, but it tends to show that long-run regrets are often about **not pursuing what felt authentic, growth-oriented, or meaningful**, rather than about not maximizing short-run rewards.

### B. Turnover / retention evidence
People are less likely to want to leave jobs that are:
- meaningful
- autonomous
- engaging
- well-matched to them

That is not the same as regret, but it is consistent with lower second-guessing.

So on regret, the evidence is suggestive rather than definitive.

---

## 8) What the research would predict in a case like yours

Speaking very impersonally and sticking to the literature:

1. **If both options already provide financial security and high status**, the extra salary is unlikely to be the dominant determinant of happiness.
2. **Day-to-day intrinsic job quality** is likely to matter a lot.
3. **Leadership desire should be counted as intrinsic**, not just extrinsic, if it is a genuine aspiration.
4. The biggest research-based risk is taking a role where the daily work is less engaging **and** the leadership component turns out not to be as satisfying as imagined.
5. If the new role gives more meaning/autonomy/impact through leading others, it may still increase satisfaction despite being “less interesting” in pure subject matter terms.

In other words, the literature suggests the key comparison is not:
- “interesting work” vs “more money”

but more like:
- **current role: intellectual interest + intrinsic engagement**
versus
- **new role: somewhat less intellectual interest, but possibly more leadership meaning / autonomy / identity fit**

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

**People generally do seem to be happier in jobs that are intrinsically engaging, meaningful, and well-matched to them, and those factors usually predict satisfaction more strongly than salary differences do once pay is already high.**

The two strongest research-backed caveats are:

- **leadership may itself be intrinsically rewarding**, if it matches who you want to be;
- **there is little direct evidence on regret** for exactly this kind of elite-career fork.

---

## Representative references
If you want to look them up:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M.** — self-determination theory in work organizations
- **Allan, B. A. et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010)** — income and well-being
- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021)** — income and experienced well-being
- **Roese, N. J., & Summerville, A. (2005)** — what people regret most

If you want, I can turn this into a **short evidence table** specifically for your decision:  
**interesting research / more pay / first-line leadership / long-term satisfaction / regret risk**, with each row tied to a paper or meta-analysis.
