answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**The intrinsic quality of the work itself** — things like interest, challenge, learning, autonomy, skill use, and meaning — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being, and often a stronger predictor than **pay level** once pay is already “good enough.”

What is **less well studied** is the exact question “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid/managerial one?” There is much more evidence on **satisfaction, well-being, burnout, turnover**, and **fit**, and those literatures are probably the best proxy.

## 1) Pay matters, but less than many people expect for job satisfaction

A robust finding in organizational psychology is that the link between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

- A well-known meta-analysis:
  - **Judge et al. (2010)** found the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** was only about **r ≈ .15**.
  - In plain English: higher pay helps, but it usually explains only a small fraction of why people like or dislike their jobs.

This does **not** mean salary is unimportant. It matters a lot when it changes:
- financial security,
- family options,
- debt/stress,
- perceived fairness,
- or relative status.

But if both options are already highly paid, the research generally suggests that **extra money alone is not a powerful predictor of day-to-day work happiness**.

## 2) Interesting/challenging work is strongly tied to satisfaction

The classic and still influential framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**:
- **Hackman & Oldham (1970s)** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:
  - **skill variety**
  - **task identity**
  - **task significance**
  - **autonomy**
  - **feedback**

This has been tested many times.

### Meta-analytic evidence
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a large body of work-design research and found that motivational job characteristics such as:
  - **autonomy**
  - **task variety**
  - **task significance**
  - **feedback**
  
  were **moderately to strongly associated** with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **intrinsic motivation**
  - **engagement**

If by “interesting” you mean “the work itself is intellectually engaging and meaningful,” that maps very closely to the constructs that predict satisfaction in this literature.

## 3) Meaningful work has a large relationship with well-being

There is now a big literature on **meaningful work**.

- Meta-analyses in this area (for example **Allan et al.**, late 2010s) generally find that **meaningful work** is associated with:
  - **higher job satisfaction**
  - **higher engagement**
  - **higher life satisfaction** (smaller than job satisfaction, but still positive)
  - **lower burnout**

The relationship with **job satisfaction** is often quite a bit larger than the pay–satisfaction relationship.

So if your current role feels substantially more interesting and meaningful than the Meta role, the literature would treat that as a **major variable**, not a soft or secondary one.

## 4) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors

One of the most relevant literatures for your situation is **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis:
  - **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** were strongly associated with:
    - **job satisfaction**
    - **organizational commitment**
    - lower **intent to quit**

This matters because the question is not just:
- “Is management better?”
- or “Is interesting work better?”

It is:
- **Which role better fits what you value and want right now?**

Research generally says that **fit** is hugely important. If leading people is something you genuinely want — not just something that sounds prestigious — then that itself is an intrinsic value, not merely an extrinsic one.

## 5) Intrinsic motives tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic motives

A related literature comes from **Self-Determination Theory**.

The basic finding is that work is better for well-being when it supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And people tend to do better psychologically when their goals are more **intrinsic**:
- growth
- mastery
- interest
- contribution

than primarily **extrinsic**:
- money
- status
- image

The most careful version of the claim is:

> Choosing primarily on intrinsic features tends to be associated with higher well-being than choosing primarily on extrinsic features, especially when basic financial needs are already met.

That is not the same as saying salary and status do not matter; they do. But the literature usually finds that **they are weaker predictors of sustained well-being** than people intuitively expect.

## 6) Promotions/leadership are more mixed than people think

There is some longitudinal research suggesting that promotions and advancement can have **mixed effects**:
- more pay
- more status
- more influence
- but also more stress, longer hours, role conflict, and less time on the core work

So the research does **not** support a simple “leadership = happier” rule.

The most evidence-based way to think about leadership is:
- it can improve satisfaction if it gives you:
  - more autonomy
  - more impact
  - more growth
  - stronger identity fit
- but it can reduce satisfaction if it mainly means:
  - less engaging work
  - more politics
  - more coordination burden
  - more stress

So the **leadership opportunity** in your case is meaningful research-wise, but not automatically positive.

## 7) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of a strong, clean body of research showing:
- “People who chose interesting work over higher pay later regretted it less,”
compared directly against the opposite choice in a causal way.

What we do have instead is indirect evidence:
- lower fit predicts later dissatisfaction and turnover
- lower meaningfulness predicts burnout and disengagement
- money predicts satisfaction somewhat, but not strongly
- intrinsic motives predict better well-being than extrinsic motives, on average

So the best research-based summary is:

> Direct “regret” evidence is limited, but the predictors of long-run satisfaction lean heavily toward interesting, meaningful, fitting work rather than marginal pay gains alone.

## 8) If I compress the literature into one practical conclusion

If two jobs are both already excellent by normal standards, the research would usually rank these as very important for happiness at work:

1. **Interest/meaning in the work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Fit with your values and strengths**
4. **Quality of relationships / team / manager**
5. **Growth and learning**
6. **Pay**, but usually less than the above once pay is already high

In your specific comparison, the literature would say:

- **“much less interesting”** is a serious negative, not a minor one.
- **“leading a small team, which I always wanted”** is also a serious positive, because it may reflect values fit and growth.
- **“a bit higher salary”** is probably the least psychologically important part of the difference, unless it materially changes your life.

## 9) Best papers / literatures to look at

If you want research anchors, these are good places to start:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on **work design**
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**
- **Hackman & Oldham** — classic job characteristics model
- **Ryan & Deci** — self-determination theory
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analyses/reviews on **meaningful work**

## Bottom line from the research

If your question is:

> “Does staying in a more interesting job usually make people happier than taking a slightly higher-paid but less interesting one?”

The research-based answer is roughly:

**Often yes, especially when compensation is already high enough and the more interesting job better fits the person.**

If the question is:

> “What could outweigh the interestingness?”

The strongest candidates are usually:
- **fit with a deeply held goal** (like genuinely wanting to lead)
- **autonomy**
- **team/manager quality**
- and sometimes **work-life impact**

If you want, I can next give you a **strictly research-based decision framework** for this exact choice — basically a 1-page scorecard using variables from the literature rather than intuition.

answer:
Yes. There is a fairly strong research literature suggesting that, **all else equal, intrinsic job qualities**—work that is interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit for your strengths—are usually **more strongly related to sustained job satisfaction and well-being** than modest differences in pay.

A few important caveats up front:

- There is **much more research on satisfaction/well-being** than on **ex post regret**.
- “Interesting work” is not one single variable in the literature; the closest constructs are:
  - **intrinsic motivation**
  - **meaningful work**
  - **job characteristics** like variety/autonomy/complexity
  - **person-job fit**
- Your tradeoff is not purely “interesting work vs money.”  
  A **leadership role** can itself be intrinsically rewarding if it fits a long-standing motive or identity.

## Main research results

### 1) Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A classic and still influential line of research is the **job characteristics model** (Hackman & Oldham), later updated in meta-analyses.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  They reviewed a large work-design literature and found that job features such as:
  - **autonomy**
  - **skill variety**
  - **task significance**
  - **feedback**
  
  were consistently associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **internal work motivation**
  - better **performance**
  - lower **burnout/strain**

The big picture from this literature is that **what you do every day** matters a lot for how good work feels.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
A more recent meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies*  
  “Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”

They found that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- greater **sense of meaning in life**
- lower **psychological distress**

This is one of the clearest modern findings relevant to your question. If by “interesting” you mean work that feels intellectually engaging and worth doing, the literature says that tends to be very important for well-being.

---

### 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect
On salary specifically:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  Meta-analysis of the relationship between **pay** and **job satisfaction**

Their headline result is often summarized as:  
- the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but fairly small** (around **.15**)

That means pay is **not irrelevant**, but compared with things like autonomy, meaning, fit, and work design, it is usually **not the dominant driver** of whether people like their jobs.

For someone already at a high income, the marginal emotional gain from “a bit higher salary” is often smaller than the gain/loss from day-to-day work quality. That is consistent with the broader subjective well-being literature too.

---

### 4) Intrinsic goals tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic goals
A separate but related literature, especially from **self-determination theory**, finds that people who prioritize **intrinsic aspirations** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) tend to do better psychologically than those who prioritize **extrinsic aspirations** (money, image, status) more heavily.

Classic papers:

- **Kasser & Ryan (1993, 1996)**  
  These studies found that placing greater relative emphasis on wealth/status/image is associated with lower well-being than emphasizing intrinsic aspirations.

This is not a direct “job offer” study, but it is highly relevant: if your choice is between a role that is more intrinsically engaging vs one that is mainly better on salary/status, the literature tends to favor the intrinsically rewarding option for well-being.

---

### 5) Person-job fit matters a lot
This may be especially relevant to your case because “leading a small team” sounds like something you genuinely want.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**

They found that fit—especially **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**—is strongly related to:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- stronger **organizational commitment**
- lower **intent to quit**

This matters because a leadership role is not merely an “extrinsic” perk. If being a manager/team lead fits your values and long-term identity, that could be a meaningful source of satisfaction even if the work content is less interesting.

So the literature would not say “always choose the more interesting technical work.”  
It would say something closer to: **choose the option with the stronger intrinsic fit overall**.

---

### 6) Promotions and advancement often give only temporary happiness boosts
There is also research suggesting that promotions/status gains can improve satisfaction, but often **less durably** than people expect.

Panel studies on promotion/job changes generally find:
- a **short-term boost** in satisfaction or positive affect
- but often **adaptation over time**
- and sometimes more **stress**, longer hours, or role strain

A useful summary source here is the broader adaptation literature, e.g.:

- **Clark, Frijters, & Shields (2008)** on adaptation and subjective well-being  
- plus panel work on promotions (e.g. **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma**)

The pattern is: **status and salary gains help, but people adapt**.  
By contrast, the quality of the daily work itself keeps showing up as important.

---

## So: do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?
**On average, yes**—if “interesting” also means things like:
- more intrinsic motivation
- more autonomy
- more skill use
- more meaning
- better fit

Those features are among the **most reliable predictors** of job satisfaction and work well-being.

## Do they regret it?
This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong literature specifically tracking:
> “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paying/promotional one—do they later regret it?”

What the nearest literature suggests is:

- People who choose paths more aligned with **intrinsic motives and fit** tend to report better well-being.
- People often **overestimate** the lasting happiness from salary/status gains.
- Promotions and higher pay often produce **adaptation**.
- So if there is a likely asymmetry, it is that people may **underweight day-to-day work quality** relative to salary/status when choosing.

But I’d say that last sentence is an inference from several literatures, not a single definitive result.

## The most defensible evidence-based summary
If I had to compress the literature into one conclusion:

> **For sustained happiness at work, intrinsic job quality (interestingness, meaning, autonomy, fit) usually matters more than modest differences in pay; leadership opportunities matter when they are themselves intrinsically valued and fit the person well.**

So in your exact case, the key research-based question is probably not:

- “interesting work vs more money?”

but:

- “which role better satisfies my core intrinsic motives over the next few years: intellectual interest, autonomy, growth, and leadership identity?”

## Best references to start with
If you want papers that are directly useful:

1. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   *Journal of Applied Psychology.*

2. **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*  
   *Journal of Management Studies.*

3. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   *Journal of Vocational Behavior.*

4. **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   *Personnel Psychology.*

5. **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M. (1993, 1996).**  
   Work on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being.

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you a **more academic mini-literature review**, with:
- stronger causal vs correlational distinctions,
- effect sizes where known,
- and findings specifically on **leadership/promotion** and well-being.

answer:
Yes. The short version from the research literature is:

**Interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work is usually a stronger predictor of job satisfaction than modest differences in pay**, especially once income is already high enough that basic security and comfort are not the binding constraint.

That’s not just folk wisdom; it shows up pretty consistently across organizational psychology and labor economics. A few specifics:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In this literature, “interesting work” is usually broken into things like:

- **skill variety / challenge**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaningfulness**
- **feedback / learning**
- **fit with your interests and values**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they involve variety, significance, identity, autonomy, and feedback.

A large meta-analysis later confirmed this:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: across many studies, those job design characteristics had **moderate to strong associations** with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions.

In plain English: people are usually happier in jobs that are genuinely engaging, allow discretion, and feel consequential.

## 2) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

There is a direct meta-analysis on this:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction is positive but modest**.

The rough takeaway from that line of work is:

- more pay helps,
- but it is **far from the dominant driver** of whether people like their jobs.

This is especially important in your kind of situation: if both options already put you well into “financially comfortable” territory, the marginal well-being gain from additional salary is usually much smaller than people intuitively expect.

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly related to well-being

Separate from “interesting,” there is literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic and review work in this area generally finds that perceived meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- less burnout / less turnover intention

A good recent anchor is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** on the outcomes of meaningful work.

This doesn’t mean meaning solves everything. It doesn’t. But as a predictor of how good work feels over time, it is one of the bigger ones.

## 4) Fit matters a lot: people do better when the job matches their interests and values

Another strong finding is from **person-job / person-organization fit** research:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit.

This matters for your question because “leading a small team” may or may not count as a positive, depending on whether it fits your motives.

If **leadership itself** is something you’ve wanted for a long time, then that is not merely an extrinsic perk — it may be an intrinsic source of meaning, competence, and identity fit.

So the tradeoff is not simply:

- interesting work vs more salary

It may be:

- technical/research interest vs leadership/identity fit

And both of those are “intrinsic” in different ways.

## 5) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central

A huge literature around **Self-Determination Theory** finds that work is better for well-being when it supports:

- **autonomy**: I have discretion / I’m not just executing
- **competence**: I’m growing / using my abilities
- **relatedness**: I’m connected to others

Reviews and meta-analytic work in this area consistently show that satisfaction of these needs predicts:

- higher engagement
- higher job satisfaction
- lower burnout
- better well-being

So if one role is more interesting but less autonomous, or less socially meaningful, that matters. The best predictor is usually not “interest” alone but the broader profile of these needs.

## 6) Economics agrees that non-monetary job attributes matter a lot

Labor economics has a related concept: **compensating differentials**.

The idea is simple: if a job is less pleasant, less flexible, more risky, more boring, etc., employers often have to pay more to attract people. That only makes sense if workers place real value on the non-monetary attributes.

There are also “willingness-to-pay” studies showing workers will accept lower wages for better job amenities such as flexibility, predictability, remote work, and other preferred working conditions.

That literature doesn’t always measure “interestingness” directly, but it strongly supports the broader point:

**people value job quality in ways that are not well summarized by salary.**

## 7) On happiness more broadly: money affects life evaluation more than day-to-day job enjoyment

The general happiness literature is relevant but should be interpreted carefully.

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income was strongly tied to **life evaluation** and, in their data, emotional well-being plateaued around a certain income level.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** later found that experienced well-being continued to rise with income on average, though not necessarily in a simple linear way.

The synthesis most people draw now is:

- money does improve well-being,
- but with **diminishing returns**,
- and its effect is often stronger on **security / life evaluation** than on whether your daily work feels engaging.

For a high-income professional choosing between two already excellent options, the literature would generally predict that **daily work quality** becomes relatively more important.

## 8) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is the weakest part of the evidence, because there is **much less direct research** on that exact question.

What we do have:

- Regret research suggests people often regret choices that violate their core values or identity.
- Career regret is commonly tied to:
  - poor fit
  - foregone growth
  - feeling one “sold out”
  - or, conversely, feeling one avoided ambition out of fear

So there is **no clean universal result** like “people regret choosing interesting work” or “people regret choosing money.”

What the evidence does suggest is that **regret tends to come more from mismatch** than from any single factor. People regret:
- taking something prestigious that didn’t fit them,
- or passing on something important to their identity.

So for your case, the key research-backed question is probably not:
- “Is interesting work more important than pay?”

but:
- “Which option better fits the motives that matter most to me over time: intellectual interest, leadership, autonomy, meaning, and financial security?”

## If you want a rough evidence-based ranking of predictors

For people already well-paid, the literature would roughly rank these as follows for **job satisfaction / day-to-day work well-being**:

1. **Meaning / interest / autonomy / challenge**
2. **Fit with values, identity, and strengths**
3. **Relationships / manager / team quality**
4. **Fair pay**
5. **Incremental extra pay beyond an already high level**

That is a simplification, but directionally it is pretty consistent with the literature.

## A few caveats

1. **Most of this evidence is correlational**
   - Happier people may perceive their work more positively.
   - But job redesign and fit research do suggest real causal effects too.

2. **“Interesting” is unstable**
   - A very interesting topic can become bureaucratic.
   - A less interesting domain can become very engaging if the role has autonomy, scope, and leadership.

3. **Leading a team is not just an external perk**
   - If leadership is a deeply held aspiration, it may itself increase meaning and satisfaction.

4. **People often mispredict what will matter**
   - Affective forecasting research suggests people tend to overweight salary, title, and prestige, and underweight day-to-day experience.

## Bottom line

If I compress the research into one sentence:

**On average, people are more satisfied in work that is interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to their values than in work that merely pays somewhat more.**

The main exceptions are when:
- the pay difference materially changes financial security,
- the “less interesting” role strongly satisfies another intrinsic goal like leadership,
- or the current interesting role has hidden costs like stagnation, bad management, or weak future fit.

## References worth looking up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980) — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007) — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010) — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005) — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** (2019) — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** — Self-Determination Theory

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**On average, people are happier in jobs that are intrinsically rewarding—interesting, meaningful, autonomous, growth-oriented—than in jobs that merely pay more.**  
And the evidence for that is fairly strong in organizational psychology and decent in labor economics.

The two main caveats are:

1. **“Interesting work” is not one thing** — it usually bundles together autonomy, skill use, challenge, meaning, learning, and sometimes status.
2. **Direct evidence on “later regret” is much thinner** than evidence on job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover.

## What the literature most consistently finds

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A very established line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later summarized in large meta-analyses.

The core finding is that jobs tend to be more satisfying and motivating when they offer:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity** (you can see a whole piece of work)
- **task significance** (it matters)
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**

found that these work-design features were **moderately to strongly associated** with job satisfaction, engagement, internal motivation, and lower withdrawal/turnover intentions.

In plain English:  
**the day-to-day texture of the work matters a lot.**

---

### 2) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction
A famous meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**

found that the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction is positive but fairly small**.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means:

- money helps,
- underpayment absolutely hurts,
- but once compensation is already strong, **differences in interest, autonomy, meaning, and manager/culture often explain more of how people feel about their job**.

So if the choice is between:
- “more interesting work”
- versus “somewhat higher salary”

the research usually leans toward **interesting work having the bigger effect on job satisfaction**, especially at already-high income levels.

---

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
There is now a sizable literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic/review evidence (for example **Allan et al.**, **Lysova et al.**) generally finds that people who experience their work as meaningful report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- better **overall well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often lower **burnout**

This is not just “having a noble mission.” In practice, meaningfulness often comes from:

- using your strengths,
- seeing impact,
- solving hard problems,
- feeling that the work matters,
- belonging to a high-quality team.

For many research-oriented people, “interesting” work is part of what makes work meaningful.

---

### 4) Autonomy is especially important
Across many frameworks—Job Characteristics Theory, Self-Determination Theory, work design research—**autonomy** is one of the most reliable predictors of good work experience.

**Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; work applications reviewed by Deci, Olafsen, Ryan and others) suggests that well-being at work is highest when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that are interesting often satisfy at least the first two:
- they let you choose how to work,
- and they let you feel capable and growing.

This is important for your case because “leading a small team” can either:
- **increase autonomy and impact**, or
- **decrease autonomy over your actual daily work** if it mostly means meetings, coordination, and less hands-on research.

The research would say: **the label “leadership” matters less than what your actual days will feel like**.

---

### 5) Person-job fit matters a lot
Another strong literature is **person-environment/person-job fit**.

Broadly, when there is good alignment between:
- your interests,
- your values,
- your strengths,
- and what the role actually demands,

people tend to have higher satisfaction, stronger commitment, and less desire to leave.

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

found that various forms of fit are meaningfully related to job satisfaction and organizational commitment.

So the best-supported question is not:
- “Is management better than research?”
but:
- **“Which role better fits what I actually enjoy, value, and want to be doing every day?”**

If you deeply value intellectually interesting work, that is not a trivial preference. In the literature, it is exactly the kind of preference that tends to predict satisfaction when honored.

---

## What about regret?

This part is less clean.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous body of research saying exactly:

> “People who chose the more interesting job later regretted it less than people who chose the higher-paying, less interesting one.”

That exact question has **much less direct evidence**.

What the nearby literature suggests is:

### A) People often overweight extrinsic factors when predicting happiness
In behavioral decision research and well-being research, people often mispredict what will matter most to their future happiness. They tend to over-focus on salient, comparable things like:

- salary,
- title,
- prestige,

and underweight:
- daily experience,
- autonomy,
- relationships,
- whether they enjoy the work itself.

That doesn’t prove regret, but it suggests a common bias.

### B) People adapt somewhat to pay
Well-being research broadly suggests that people often **adapt** to income increases faster than they expect. The extra pay becomes normal.  
By contrast, if your everyday work is boring or energizing, that tends to keep affecting you because it is repeatedly experienced.

So if you’re asking, “Which choice is more likely to matter six months later, once novelty wears off?” the evidence usually points to:
- **daily work content**
- **autonomy**
- **social environment**
more than modest compensation differences.

### C) Regret seems tied to misfit
The career-regret literature is smaller and messier, but one common theme is that regret tends to be higher when people feel they chose based on external rewards while moving away from their values/interests.

That is more of a recurring pattern than a single canonical result.

---

## How leadership changes the equation

This is the part where the research becomes more conditional.

You said that leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.” That matters a lot.

Because for many people, leadership adds intrinsic rewards too:

- more impact
- mentoring
- broader influence
- more autonomy over direction
- identity growth

But for other people it removes the things they actually love:

- deep technical work
- flow
- problem solving
- craftsmanship
- uninterrupted thinking

So if the Meta role is “less interesting” only in the narrow sense of **less individual technical research**, but more meaningful because it gives you leadership, impact, and ownership, the tradeoff is not simply “interesting vs money.”

From the research lens, it becomes something like:

- current job: stronger **intrinsic task interest**
- proposed job: maybe stronger **autonomy/impact/leadership identity**, but weaker **technical interest**

And those are all meaningful predictors of satisfaction.

---

## If you want the most research-grounded takeaway

The best-supported summary is:

### People are usually happiest when their job gives them:
1. **interesting/challenging work**
2. **autonomy**
3. **a sense of competence/growth**
4. **meaning/impact**
5. **good colleagues/manager**
6. **fair pay**

If pay is already high enough that you are not financially constrained, then **differences in 1–5 usually matter more for job satisfaction than additional pay**.

So, on the narrow research question:

- **Do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?**  
  **Yes, on average, very likely.** The evidence is fairly strong.

- **Do they regret staying in the more interesting job?**  
  **Direct evidence is weaker**, but the adjacent evidence suggests that people often regret value-misaligned choices more than they regret passing up incremental pay.

- **Is interesting work more important than other factors?**  
  Often yes, but not alone. It works together with autonomy, meaning, fit, and relationships.

---

## The most relevant papers/lines of research to look at

If you want a reading list, these are good anchors:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Theory
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design/job characteristics
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis
- **Deci & Ryan / Deci, Olafsen, Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory at work
- **Allan et al.** and **Lysova et al.** — meaningful work outcomes/reviews

---

If you want, I can do a **more scholarly version next**: a compact literature review with specific papers, what each found, and which ones are strongest methodologically.

answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology is:

**More interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.**  
**Higher pay helps, but its effect on job satisfaction is usually much smaller.**  
**Promotions/leadership roles are mixed: they can increase status, autonomy, and meaning, but also stress and workload.**  
**On regret, direct career-choice evidence is thinner, but long-run regret research suggests people often regret passing on identity-relevant opportunities more than passing on extra money.**

A more research-grounded summary:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” matters a lot

In the work-design literature, what people call “interesting work” usually maps onto things like:

- skill variety
- autonomy
- task significance
- feedback
- opportunities to learn/grow
- meaningfulness

A major meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  They meta-analyzed hundreds of studies on work design.  
  **Core job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback had moderate-to-strong associations with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.**

This is very consistent with the older:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** job characteristics model  
  Their theory, supported by a large later literature, is that work is more satisfying when it is experienced as meaningful, allows responsibility/autonomy, and provides knowledge of results.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I’m engaged by the actual substance of the work,” that is not a trivial preference; it is a central variable in the literature.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation usually predicts well-being better than extrinsic rewards

A related body of work comes from **self-determination theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan** and subsequent research
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** meta-analytic work on basic psychological needs at work

The robust finding is that jobs are better for well-being when they satisfy needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often supports **autonomy + competence** particularly strongly.

This literature generally finds that when work is intrinsically motivating, people show:

- higher engagement
- higher job satisfaction
- lower burnout
- better general well-being

So if one option gives you much stronger day-to-day intrinsic motivation, research would treat that as a serious advantage, not a “soft” factor.

---

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction

There is also a separate “meaningful work” literature:

- e.g. reviews/meta-analyses by **Allan** and colleagues, and broader reviews such as **Rosso et al. (2010)**

The general pattern is:

- people who experience their work as meaningful report **higher job satisfaction**
- often also **higher life satisfaction / sense of meaning**
- and **lower psychological distress**

If your current work feels unusually important or intellectually alive, that likely contributes real well-being beyond compensation.

---

## 4) Pay matters, but less than many people think for job satisfaction

The best-known result here:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

Their conclusion was that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. In plain English:

- more pay does tend to increase satisfaction
- but the effect is **much smaller** than people often assume
- and usually smaller than job-content variables like autonomy, interestingness, fit, and meaning

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that once basic financial concerns are met, differences in day-to-day work quality often matter more for how happy people are in the job.

At very high income levels, the marginal well-being gain from “a bit higher salary” tends to be especially hard to distinguish from noise compared with a major change in what you do all day.

Related broader well-being work:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)**

That literature is more about income and general well-being than job choice specifically, but the big picture is:
- money does improve life evaluation
- experienced well-being also tends to improve, though with diminishing practical impact per extra dollar at higher incomes
- income differences do not fully compensate for poor daily experience

---

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another robust result:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on person-environment fit

They found that **person-job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction and related outcomes.

This is important because the literature does **not** say “interesting jobs are best for everyone in the same way.” It says:
- outcomes are best when the job fits what the person values

So if **you** highly value intellectually interesting work, then giving it up is likely more costly for your well-being than it would be for someone who mostly values compensation or organizational status.

Conversely, if **leading a small team** is a deep, long-standing aspiration, that is also an intrinsic fit variable, not merely an extrinsic one.

That distinction matters.

---

## 6) Promotions / leadership roles: mixed evidence

The evidence on promotions and managerial responsibility is not as uniformly positive as people assume.

Typical findings from panel studies:
- promotions often raise **pay satisfaction** and sometimes **job satisfaction in the short term**
- but gains often **partly fade**
- promotions can increase:
  - stress
  - work hours
  - role conflict
  - administrative burden

So “leading a team” is not automatically better or worse for well-being. It depends on whether the role gives you:
- autonomy
- impact
- growth
- social meaning

or instead mostly gives you:
- meetings
- performance management
- politics
- less time on the work you actually enjoy

Research would tell you to decompose “lead a team” into its job characteristics, not treat it as pure upside.

---

## 7) What about regret?

This is the least directly answered by the job literature, but there is relevant general research.

Classic result:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** on regret  
  Over time, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions.

Later regret research refines this:
- long-run regret is especially strong when the missed opportunity is tied to the **ideal self** — the person one wanted to become

Applied to your case, research would suggest:

- People often do **not** deeply regret passing up a somewhat higher salary.
- They are **more likely** to regret passing up something that feels central to identity:
  - “doing the most interesting work of my career”
  - or “finally getting to lead a team”

So the regret question is not mainly “interesting work vs money.”  
It is more likely:

> Which forgone option would feel more like betraying my ideal self?

The literature suggests that kind of missed, identity-relevant path can generate durable regret.

---

## 8) A subtle but important point: you may be comparing two different kinds of intrinsic value

Your description is not just:

- **interesting work** vs **higher salary**

It may be:

- **interesting technical/research content**
  vs
- **leadership/ownership/development as a manager**

Research would classify both as potentially **intrinsic** goods.

So the question is not necessarily “intrinsic vs extrinsic.”  
It may be “which intrinsic motive is stronger for you?”

For example:

- If your strongest driver is **intellectual engagement / frontier research / novelty**, then the OpenAI job likely scores better on the dimensions most associated with satisfaction.
- If your strongest driver is **autonomy, influence, building, coaching, and growing into leadership**, then the Meta role may score better on those dimensions — even if the domain is less fascinating.

That is exactly the kind of nuance the work-design and person-job-fit literatures would predict.

---

## 9) So, do people who stay in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?

**On average, yes** — if “interesting” means richer job design, more meaning, more skill use, more autonomy, and better fit.

That is one of the more robust findings in the field.

But two caveats:

1. **A lot of the evidence is correlational**, though the pattern is strong and supported by longitudinal work too.  
2. “Interesting” is multidimensional. A less interesting domain can still be a better-fit job if it provides autonomy, leadership, and growth you deeply value.

---

## 10) If I had to compress the research into one sentence

**For job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being, the content and quality of the work usually matter more than a modest salary increase; but regret is most likely around whichever path you see as central to the person you want to become.**

---

## A few concrete references to look up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary. *Journal of Applied Psychology*.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, etc.  
- **Van den Broeck, A., et al. (2016).** A review/meta-analysis of basic psychological need satisfaction at work.  
- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010).** On the meaning of work.  
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1995).** The experience of regret: What, when, and why.  
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010).** High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.  
- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021)** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)** on income and experienced well-being.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision framework based explicitly on the literature** — e.g. a 6-factor scorecard using autonomy, meaning, learning, fit, compensation, and regret risk.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

- **Interesting/intrinsically motivating work is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect**, especially once compensation is already high enough that money is not solving a real constraint.
- **Regret evidence is thinner and less direct**, but the best-known findings suggest people often regret **foregone growth/self-defining opportunities** more than incremental financial differences.

A research-based summary:

## 1) “Interesting work” really does matter a lot

The classic and still influential line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1975, 1976)** proposed that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:
  - **skill variety**
  - **task identity**
  - **task significance**
  - **autonomy**
  - **feedback**

“Interesting work” usually overlaps especially with **skill variety**, **challenge**, **significance**, and **feedback**.

This model has held up reasonably well in later reviews and meta-analyses:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)**: meta-analytic support that enriched job characteristics are associated with higher internal motivation and job satisfaction.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: a large meta-analysis showing that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback** have substantial relationships with:
  - job satisfaction
  - engagement
  - performance
  - lower turnover intentions

### Bottom line:
If one job is genuinely more intellectually engaging, varied, and absorbing, the literature would generally predict **higher day-to-day job satisfaction** in that role.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation tends to be better for well-being than extrinsic rewards alone

A second major framework is **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** for work settings

This literature finds that people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy** (“I choose / I have agency”)
- **competence** (“I’m growing / mastering hard things”)
- **relatedness** (“I’m connected to people I value”)

Interesting work often supports **competence** and sometimes autonomy. Leadership opportunities can support **autonomy, competence, and relatedness** too, depending on the environment.

The key finding from this tradition is that **intrinsic motivation** and need satisfaction are reliably associated with:
- greater job satisfaction
- better well-being
- better persistence and performance

So if your current work feels more interesting because it is more aligned with curiosity, mastery, and intrinsic motivation, that is not a soft or sentimental factor. It is very well supported in the literature.

---

## 3) Pay does matter — but usually less than people think

The strongest evidence here is that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is real but **modest**.

A key paper:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

The broad result: **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction, but the effect is fairly small** relative to many intrinsic and social job factors.

Related broader well-being work:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: income strongly affects life evaluation, but emotional well-being shows diminishing returns.
- **Killingsworth (2021/2023)** complicates the exact “plateau” story, but still doesn’t overturn the basic practical point that **marginal increases in already-high income are usually not the dominant determinant of experienced well-being**.

### Practical translation:
If Meta is offering “a bit higher salary,” research would generally predict that **a modest pay increase alone** is unlikely to offset a **large drop in interest/intrinsic motivation**, unless that extra money materially changes something important in your life.

---

## 4) Job fit matters a lot

Another strong finding: it is not only the objective features of the job, but whether the job fits **you**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit

Better fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to quit
- better overall attitudes

This matters for your case because “leading a small team” is not just an external perk — if it is something you’ve “always wanted to do,” that may reflect a genuine **self-concordant goal**.

Relevant research:
- **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)** on **self-concordance**: people are happier and more persistent when they pursue goals aligned with their deeper interests and values.

So your choice is not simply:
- **interesting work** vs **money**

It is more like:
- **research interest / intellectual absorption**
vs
- **leadership aspiration / growth into a valued identity**

Both of those are intrinsic in different ways.

---

## 5) Job satisfaction does spill into overall life satisfaction

This matters because one might say: maybe interesting work affects only “job satisfaction,” not actual happiness.

But the literature generally finds a **moderate positive relationship** between job satisfaction and life satisfaction / subjective well-being.

Examples:
- classic meta-analytic work such as **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)**
- later syntheses like **Bowling, Eschleman, & Wang (2010)**

So yes: on average, people who are more satisfied with their work tend also to be happier overall. Not perfectly, but meaningfully.

Since work occupies a huge fraction of waking life, this is not surprising.

---

## 6) On regret specifically: evidence is less direct, but there are some useful findings

This part of the literature is weaker for your exact question.

I don’t know of a clean, definitive body of research that says:

> “People who choose the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership job later regret it less.”

That exact comparison is hard to study causally.

But there are two relevant lines:

### A. Long-term regret tends to favor inactions over actions
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the short run, people often regret actions more; in the long run, they more often regret **inactions** — the paths not taken.

This suggests that if the team-lead opportunity feels like a major unrealized aspiration, declining it could become a salient long-run regret source.  
But that is an inference, not a direct finding about your exact case.

### B. Regrets often center on the “ideal self”
Later regret research also suggests that long-term regrets disproportionately involve failing to become the person one wanted to become. Career and educational regrets often fit this pattern.

So if “I wanted to build and lead a small research/engineering team” is part of your ideal self, that matters psychologically in a way that “slightly higher comp” usually does not.

---

## 7) Important caveat: interesting work is not enough if the rest of the job is bad

Interesting work predicts better outcomes, but there are strong moderators:

### Other factors with large effects:
- **manager quality**
- **autonomy**
- **workload / hours**
- **role clarity**
- **organizational politics**
- **coworker quality**
- **psychological safety**
- **work-life conflict**

There is good stress research showing that challenge can be energizing, but chronic overload and lack of control are damaging.

So if the “more interesting” job also means:
- chronic overwork
- low autonomy
- instability
- poor management
then its advantage can shrink or reverse.

Similarly, a team-lead role may be “less intellectually interesting” in one sense but higher in:
- autonomy
- influence
- meaning
- growth
- identity fit

That can matter a lot.

---

# What the literature would predict in a case like yours

If I strip this down to the research-supported core:

### If the Meta role is:
- only **slightly** higher paid
- **much less interesting**
- and doesn’t clearly improve autonomy / manager quality / fit / life constraints

then the literature would generally predict that **staying in the more interesting role is more likely to maximize day-to-day job satisfaction and probably overall well-being**.

### But if the Meta role gives you:
- a genuinely valued **leadership opportunity**
- more **autonomy**
- more **scope/influence**
- better long-term fit with the person you want to become

then the tradeoff is not “interesting vs money”; it becomes:
- **research-interest fit**
vs
- **leadership-growth fit**

And the literature does support the importance of both.

---

# The highest-confidence research takeaways

If I had to compress the evidence into a few claims:

1. **Interesting, challenging, autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a positive but relatively modest effect on job satisfaction.**
3. **Fit with your values/identity matters a lot.**
4. **Job satisfaction meaningfully affects overall well-being.**
5. **Long-term regret often comes from paths not taken, especially those tied to one’s ideal self.**
6. **There is no simple universal rule; leadership opportunity can itself be an intrinsic good, not just an extrinsic one.**

---

# Papers / research traditions worth reading

If you want the actual literature, these are good anchors:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987).** The validity of the job characteristics model: A review and meta-analysis.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).** Self-determination theory and work motivation.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.
- **Bowling, N. A., Eschleman, K. J., & Wang, Q. (2010).** A meta-analytic examination of the relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being.
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1995).** The experience of regret: What, when, and why.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision framework explicitly tied to the literature** — e.g. a weighted scorecard with dimensions like:
- intrinsic interest
- leadership growth
- autonomy
- fit with ideal self
- manager/team quality
- workload
- compensation
- future option value

That would stay research-grounded rather than opinion-based.

answer:
Yes. The short version of the research is:

**Interesting, intrinsically rewarding work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and a meaningful contributor to overall well-being.**  
**Pay matters too, but once compensation is already high enough, the marginal well-being benefit of “more money” is usually smaller than the benefit of better intrinsic job quality** — things like interest, autonomy, skill use, growth, and meaning.

A more careful summary:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is not a vague soft factor in the literature — it maps to well-studied constructs

In organizational psychology, what you’re calling “interesting” usually overlaps with:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job meaningfulness**
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **learning/growth**
- **person-job fit**

These have been studied for decades.

### Classic framework: Job Characteristics Model
Hackman & Oldham’s work showed that jobs tend to be more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- variety
- identity/ownership
- significance
- autonomy
- feedback

That was later backed by larger meta-analytic work.

### Meta-analysis: work design predicts satisfaction and motivation
A very cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, on work design

They found that work characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, social support, and feedback** were **reliably associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower strain/burnout**.

**Interpretation:** jobs that are more interesting and agentic are not just “nicer”; they systematically predict better outcomes.

---

## 2) Meaningful / interesting work tends to matter more for satisfaction than pay level

There is fairly consistent evidence that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**, while intrinsic job qualities have a stronger one.

### Pay and job satisfaction: positive, but modest
A key review/meta-analytic result is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction

The relationship between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with their jobs** is **positive but relatively small**.

That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It means that, statistically, **salary explains less of job satisfaction than many people intuitively think**, especially compared with day-to-day job experience.

### Meaningful work: stronger associations
Reviews/meta-analyses on **meaningful work** generally find moderate-to-strong links with:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- life meaning
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions

Examples include work by:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay**
- **Steger and colleagues**

The exact effect sizes vary by study and measurement, but the broad pattern is robust:
**people who experience their work as meaningful/interesting are usually happier at work than those who don’t, even after accounting for pay and status.**

---

## 3) Intrinsic motivation predicts well-being better than extrinsic motivation

A large body of research from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) is very relevant here.

### Self-Determination Theory (SDT)
SDT argues that people flourish when work supports three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that are interesting often satisfy at least the first two strongly.

Research in SDT consistently finds that:

- **autonomous/intrinsic motivation** is associated with higher well-being, persistence, and performance
- **controlled/extrinsic motivation** (money, status, external pressure) is weaker as a basis for sustained satisfaction

A useful summary source is:

- **Van den Broeck et al.** on basic psychological needs at work
- broader Deci & Ryan reviews/meta-analyses

**Interpretation:** if the OpenAI role is highly interesting because it feels intellectually alive, self-directed, and competence-building, research would treat that as a major well-being asset.

---

## 4) People who orient more toward intrinsic goals tend to report higher well-being than people who prioritize extrinsic goals

This is slightly different from job-choice studies, but very relevant.

### Intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and others shows that people who prioritize:

- growth
- relationships
- community
- meaningful activity

tend to do better on well-being than those who heavily prioritize:

- money
- image
- status

There’s also meta-analytic work linking **materialistic/extrinsic value orientation** to **lower well-being** on average.

Important nuance:
- this does **not** mean money/status are bad
- it means that **when they crowd out intrinsic motives**, people often end up less satisfied than expected

That’s very close to your situation: the move is not purely “more money,” but the tradeoff includes **more extrinsic reward/status** versus **less interesting work**.

Research tends to favor the intrinsic side for long-run well-being.

---

## 5) “Interesting” work also relates to overall life satisfaction, not just job satisfaction

There’s a long literature showing that **job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction**.

People do not compartmentalize perfectly. Since work occupies so much time and attention, the quality of work often affects:

- mood outside work
- stress levels
- sense of purpose
- general life satisfaction

So even if the question is “which job makes people happier overall?”, not just “which job do they like more?”, the literature still tends to assign substantial weight to intrinsic job quality.

This is one reason “interesting work” matters so much: it affects your **daily experience**, not just your résumé.

---

## 6) Person-job fit matters a lot — including fit with leadership

This is one place where your case gets more nuanced.

You’re not choosing between:
- interesting work
vs.
- boring work + cash

You’re also choosing:
- individual-contributor/research path
vs.
- leading a team, which you say you’ve “always wanted”

That matters because **fit** is itself strongly predictive of satisfaction.

### Person-environment / person-job fit
Meta-analyses such as:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

show that **fit** between person and role/organization is associated with higher:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- better well-being

So if team leadership is a deeply valued part of your identity and goals, that may itself be an intrinsic good, not merely a status bump.

In other words:
- **“interesting technical work”** is one intrinsic factor
- **“getting to lead and build a team”** may be another intrinsic factor

Research would say: what matters is not the label, but which job better satisfies your core psychological motives.

---

## 7) Promotions / leadership roles do not reliably increase happiness as much as people expect

There is research suggesting that promotions and higher-status roles often bring:

- more income
- more control in some respects
- more stress
- more hours
- more administrative burden
- sometimes no lasting gain in overall well-being

The literature here is less clean than the literature on intrinsic motivation, but the broad pattern is:

**promotion is not automatically a well-being win**  
and  
**higher-status roles can be disappointing if they reduce the work people actually enjoy**

This is especially true when a role gives nominal status but less of:
- the core task the person loves
- autonomy over how they spend time
- flow/engagement

So the “small-team lead” aspect could be fulfilling — or could become coordination, hiring, performance management, and politics. Research would say that the answer depends heavily on whether that leadership role actually delivers autonomy/impact or mainly adds strain.

---

## 8) On regret specifically: the evidence is thinner, but it points in a similar direction

There’s less direct research on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the better-paid less interesting one?” as a clean question.

What the literature does show is:

### a) Affective forecasting errors
People often mispredict what will make them happy.
They tend to **overweight salient extrinsic factors** (money, prestige, title) and **underweight everyday experience**.

So people often think:
- “higher salary + leadership title will feel great”

but then adapt to those gains, while the daily experience of the work matters more than expected.

### b) Regret is higher when choices conflict with core values / identity
Research on self-concordance and value alignment suggests that people feel better about choices that reflect their deeper motives and identity.

So if someone chooses an option that looks objectively impressive but is misaligned with what they genuinely enjoy, they are more likely to later feel:
- drained
- trapped
- “why did I leave the thing I loved?”

That’s not just folk wisdom; it aligns with self-concordance research.

### c) Extrinsic-goal prioritization predicts worse long-run well-being
That doesn’t prove regret, but it is the closest broad finding:
people who optimize too strongly for external markers often end up less satisfied than expected.

---

## 9) How strong is the evidence, really?

The evidence is **strong** that:

- intrinsic job qualities predict job satisfaction
- meaningful/autonomous work predicts better well-being
- pay is only a modest predictor of job satisfaction
- person-job fit matters a lot

The evidence is **moderate** that:

- choosing more extrinsic rewards over intrinsic satisfaction often leads to lower long-term well-being than expected
- leadership/promotion does not reliably increase happiness unless it improves fit/autonomy/meaning

The evidence is **weaker / less direct** on:

- explicit long-term regret after choosing “interesting job” vs “higher-paid less interesting job”

That exact decision has not, as far as I know, been studied in a neat randomized or canonical longitudinal way.

---

## 10) What the research would imply for your specific tradeoff

Sticking strictly to the literature, the key variables are probably:

1. **How much more money is “a bit higher”?**
   - If both jobs are already in a very high-compensation range, the marginal happiness gain from extra salary is likely limited.

2. **How much less interesting is “much less interesting”?**
   - If the new role substantially reduces curiosity, flow, learning, or meaning, that likely matters a lot.

3. **Is “leading a small team” intrinsically meaningful to you, or mostly attractive as a milestone/status signal?**
   - If leadership itself is deeply energizing, that changes the calculus.
   - If it is mainly symbolic, research would be more skeptical.

4. **Will the Meta role actually increase autonomy and impact, or mostly increase coordination/stress?**
   - Leadership helps when it brings meaningful control and growth.
   - It hurts when it replaces engaging work with friction.

5. **Which role better fits your identity over the next few years?**
   - Fit is a major predictor of satisfaction.

---

## Bottom-line research answer

If you force me to summarize the literature in one sentence:

> **On average, people are happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomy-supportive than in jobs that merely pay somewhat more or look more impressive.**

A second sentence is needed to make that fair:

> **However, if the leadership role is itself intrinsically meaningful to you — not just higher-status — then it may also score highly on the same psychological dimensions that make work satisfying.**

So the research does **not** say “always choose the more interesting technical job.”  
It says more like:

- **Intrinsic job quality usually beats marginal extrinsic gains**
- **But leadership can itself be an intrinsic gain if it truly fits you**

---

## If you want actual papers/authors to look up

A good short reading list would be:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction
- **Deci & Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Van den Broeck et al.** — basic psychological needs at work
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis
- **Steger et al.** — meaningful work
- **Allan et al.** — meaningful work meta-analytic/review work
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations

If you want, I can give you:
1. a **more rigorous literature summary with citations**, or  
2. a **decision framework based only on these research findings**.
